# Extracao: transcription_mp4_01-The_Best_Storytellers_Have_the_Most_Stories

- **Fonte:** transcription_mp4_01-The_Best_Storytellers_Have_the_Most_Stories.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0.01 MB

---

Before I teach you to tell better stories, we have to find the right stories to tell, because here's the truth. Content is king. The best storytellers in the world are the people who have the most stories to tell. Here's the real truth. I would rather hear the right story told poorly than the wrong story told well. And the world is filled with people telling the wrong stories well. We have to have a lot of content in order to be great storytellers. We have to have a multitude of stories to tell. In fact, what I would argue is that finding stories is more important than telling better stories. Finding stories to tell, filling your mind and filling your heart with lots of things that can connect to people and move people and make them laugh and cry. Having lots and lots and lots of things to say is more important than telling them well. And so for this module, we're going to be talking about the ways in which I want you to find stories to tell. Find your best stories to tell. When you become a person who has more stories to tell than you have time to tell them, that is a beautiful problem to have. That is what I want for you. Because when you become a storyteller who has something new to say every time they meet someone, you become an example machine. You become a metaphor machine. You become the kind of person that regardless of the scenario, regardless of the situation, regardless of the person you're talking to, you will always have something to say. Something meaningful, something connective, something moving, something relevant, something that people want to hear. And so over the course of this module, I'm going to teach you strategies and tricks and methods that you can use to begin finding stories in your everyday life. And I want you doing it right away. I want you to begin collecting stories immediately because every day that you live and you don't collect a story, you lose a story. I promise you that. So as you learn a strategy, begin using it immediately. I also want you to trust me when I say that you have lots of stories to tell. Many, many stories to tell. You have small stories and big stories. And most importantly, you have stories that people want to hear. I think one of the tragedies of this world is that people move through the world filled with stories to tell, believing that no one wants to hear them or that they're not worth telling. Years ago, I was doing a workshop at a yoga retreat center. It was a week-long workshop. And the deal of this workshop was you didn't have to speak if you didn't want to. And so lots of those storytellers would stand up and tell lots of stories. But over the course of the week, one woman didn't say a word. She sat quietly, took lots of notes, asked lots of questions of storytellers, but never told a story herself. On the very last day, in the very last hour of that workshop, she was sitting directly to my right and people were telling stories. And finally she whispered to me, she said, can I just tell something? It's not actually a story and I don't know if it's any good, but I'd like to tell it. And I said, yes, please tell us the thing you want to tell us. And so she told us a story about a night when she was doing a yoga session late at night, trying to find centered calmness in her life. She was a person who was prone to angry outbursts and she was using yoga to help her relax and control those angry outbursts. She had spent three hours meditating and doing all the things that yoga people do. And then she got in her car and drove home, feeling at peace. As she's driving down the highway, a darkened highway in the Berkshire mountains, a car comes up behind her, barreling, coming right up on her. And as she's driving, she's thinking, I'm calm, I'm peaceful, I'm not going to get angry, I'm not going to let this affect me. And the car gets closer and closer, starts swerving in the lane behind her. I'm calm, I'm peaceful, I'm not going to let this bother me. The car swings around and cuts her off. And suddenly she's angry, she's outraged, she's swearing. All the work she has just done at the yoga studio is gone and she's right back to being angry. And as she's yelling at this guy behind the wheel of this car, she watches as the car drifts off and runs into a tree, head on. And she can't believe it. So she pulls up behind this car, she jumps out of her car, she runs over and she finds the man behind the wheel very badly injured. And she calls 911. And the 911 operator says, it's going to be a little bit before we can get an ambulance to her. We need you to start doing medical work on this guy right away. We need you to start treating this man. We're going to put a doctor on the phone and you need to start helping this man. And so she does. On the phone, she begins treating this stranger who she doesn't know. The ambulance arrives, they take the man out of the car and they ask the woman, they say, will you follow us to the hospital? Because you did some treatment on the man and the doctors might want to know what you did. So grudgingly, she agrees. She follows the ambulance to the hospital. She's in that little waiting room just outside the emergency room. She's waiting to see if any of the doctors need to ask her any questions, hoping she can go home soon when her best friend walks into the emergency room. She looks at her best friend and she says, what are you doing here? And she says, my husband was in a car accident. The woman had never met her friend's husband. Her friend's husband worked late night shifts and slept during the day. It turns out the man behind the wheel was her best friend's husband and he was having a heart attack behind the wheel, which is why he was swerving all over the road. She was giving medical treatment to her best friend's husband and she had no idea. The man dies and the woman says to her friend, in the last few moments of my husband's life, I'm so glad that you were the person with him. I'm so glad someone that I love was with him in his final moments. And then she turns to the class and says, do you think anyone would want to hear that story? We were all crying at that point. But that's what happens to people. We're filled with stories like that. And we walk through our lives thinking people don't want to listen to us. Mostly because people don't listen to us. Mostly because very few people take the time to listen to one another. But if you become a good storyteller and you find things to say and then you learn how to say them well, people will listen to you. But I want you to know that those things that are already in your heart and mind, those stories you've been holding on to that you don't think someone wants to hear, they deserve to be heard and there are people who want to hear them. Not only do people want to hear them, but we can take those stories and we can begin to use them to our own benefit. We can find ways to connect to people with these stories. We can move them. We can convince them. We can make them trust us. You don't need to have a story like that lady had in order to be a great storyteller. Small stories are beautiful too. But as you start to look at the stories in your life and you start to wonder, would anyone want to listen to my story? They will, I promise you. So we're going to begin finding stories in your everyday life. And what I want you to do is to become convinced that people want to hear those stories, that they're important, that they're meaningful and they deserve to be heard. Now let's go find some stories.
